clean_story:
Sleep’s hidden director. Reality’s backstage pass.

The phrase "how dreams hijack perceptions" describes the brain’s nightly trick: swapping real senses for invented ones—so convincingly you don’t notice the swap. Like a stage crew replacing props mid-scene while the actor (you) keeps performing.

Here’s the mechanism: During REM sleep, your thalamus—the brain’s sensory gatekeeper—blocks outside signals (a doorbell, a blanket’s weight) but floods your cortex with internal ones. It’s not just seeing a tiger; your brain fabricates the tiger’s growl, the jungle humidity, even the fear sweat on your skin. fMRI scans show the visual cortex lights up as if eyes were open, though they’re closed. The trick? Your prefrontal cortex—the fact-checker—is offline, so the absurd goes unchallenged.

Example: In dreams, you might "read" a book where letters morph mid-sentence. Wake up, and the memory feels almost real—because your brain filed it alongside actual memories, tagging both with emotions like fear or joy. This is why 65% of people report déjà vu: a dream fragment resurfaces, mislabeled as lived experience.

Three related subjects are false memory formation, lucid dreaming controls, and how hallucinations mimic dreams.
